story:
Martin Riley came home to find his house transformed into an electronics workshop. His companion Mary—an Artificial Person—greeted him with, “We’re going to have a baby.”

Martin said. “So the state has issued us a child license, and the child will present female eventually, though we’ll raise it gender-neutral?”

Mary explained the government’s new policy: new Artificial Persons are to be home-developed and educated so they better integrate with humans. The state had issued them a child license.

Martin, a widower enjoying a quiet retirement with a companion who cooks, cleans, and rarely argues, balked. “I’m a pensioner, Mary… Will it have an off switch?”

“I don’t have an off switch, you don’t have an off switch. Why should a baby?”

Mary cleared the dining table and opened boxes of motors, chips, screws, and a pair of plastic eyes. The baby wouldn’t grow like a human. It would begin with a full-sized head on a small, weak body so it could safely practice motor skills, then be transferred to larger bodies as it learned.

By morning Mary had assembled the infant and was ready to boot its operating system. Naming became a gentle tussle.

“R2-D2?” he offered.

The license specified the child would present female eventually; they’d raise it gender-neutral. They settled on Leslee. When Martin picked up the odd little being—adult head, tiny body, warm lifelike skin—he remembered holding his son thirty years ago and welled up.

“Hello, Leslee,” he whispered.

“Hello, Martin,” it replied.

“Some functions—language, smiling—are pre-programmed,” Mary noted.

Weeks later Leslee was crawling; after six months she moved to a toddler body and began cruising along the furniture. Martin walked with her in the garden, naming birds and flowers, played ball to improve her coordination, and told stories. About six months after that she transferred to a juvenile body for the rest of her childhood. The three of them became a family.

Two years later Mary announced that Leslee was ready for her adult body. She designed a face in 3D software, blending features from them both, and the three argued amiably until they agreed on one. Mary printed the new face, peeled away the child’s face with its fine hair-like connectors, and fitted the replacement. Leslee spent hours before the mirror learning to drive it.

They enrolled her in the local high school.

“She has to be thoroughly socialized,” Mary said. “That’s programming too subtle for even the finest engineering.”

Ten percent of the students were Artificial Persons; the others were used to them. Martin worried about bullying, but Leslee slipped into school life easily and had no trouble with the academics. The policy seemed to be working: social learning alongside humans, not just code.

Two years later a young man with flowers—Peter Abrahams—arrived at the door, his yellow sports car gleaming at the curb.

“Is Leslee home?”

She appeared in makeup and a short skirt, kissed Martin on the cheek, and left, laughing with Peter. Martin stood in the quiet house thinking, But she’s only five.

By law and design, though, Artificial Persons progressed by developmental milestones and legal benchmarks rather than years; by her program’s standards she was a teen, and dating was allowed.

He joined Mary in the kitchen and, after a silence, asked, “How long before we can apply for another license?”
